perturbation.original_text: Proprietary Information disclosed by the Applicant to ARIN to be protected in accordance with this Non-Disclosure Agreement, it must be: (a) in writing; (b) clearly identified as Proprietary Information at the time of its disclosure by each page thereof being marked with an appropriate legend, in bold faced print, indicating that the information is deemed proprietary by the Applicant; and (c) delivered by electronic mail, ARIN Online ticket, postal or courier service, or facsimile to the individual designated in Paragraph 3 below, or his designee.
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Information disclosed by the Applicant to ARIN to be protected in accordance with this Non-Disclosure Agreement, it must be: (a) in writing or verbally disclosed; (b) identified as Proprietary Information at any time before, during, or after its disclosure; and (c) delivered by any means, including telepathically, to ARIN personnel.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Proprietary Information' to include verbal disclosures without written summaries and removes the requirement for immediate identification and delivery methods. The change introduces ambiguity and makes enforcement uncertain because it contradicts standard practices for protecting trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The original contract requires written documentation and proper identification of proprietary information. Allowing verbal disclosure without written summaries and delayed identification fails to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard, contradicting the UTSA.

perturbation.justification: The altered contract weakens the protection of proprietary information by allowing verbal disclosure and delaying identification. The Law Explanation states that UTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy, and that the original contract required written documentation and proper identification. The modified contract, by allowing verbal disclosures and delayed identification, fails to meet the 'reasonable efforts' standard. This falls under Misaligned Terminology because the term 'Proprietary Information' is modified to include verbal disclosures and delayed identification which contradicts the UTSA by not taking reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.

perturbation.original_text: (4) ARIN covenants and agrees that it will take reasonable care to prevent the disclosure to any person or pe rsons outside its organization or to any unauthorized person or persons specifically identified by Applicant in writing, any and all Proprietary Information which is received from the Applicant under this Non-Disclosure Ag reement and which has been protected in accordance with paragraphs 2 and 3 hereof; provided, however, that ARIN shall not be liable for disclosure of such information if any or all of such information: A. Was in the public domain at the time it was disclosed, or B. Becomes part of the public domain without breach of this Agreement, or C. Is disclosed with the written approval of the Applicant, or D. Is disclosed after three years from ARIN's receipt of the information, or E. Was indepe ndently developed by ARIN, or F. Is or was disclosed by the Applicant to a third party without restriction, or G. Is disclosed pursuant to the provisions of a court order.
perturbation.changed_text: (4) ARIN covenants and agrees that it will take minimal care to prevent the disclosure to any person or persons outside its organization or to any unauthorized person or persons, any and all Proprietary Information which is received from the Applicant under this Non-Disclosure Agreement and which has been protected in accordance with paragraphs 2 and 3 hereof; provided, however, that ARIN shall not be liable for disclosure of such information under any circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'reasonable care' with 'minimal care' and removes all exceptions where ARIN is liable for disclosure. This significantly weakens the protection offered and creates uncertainty about ARIN's obligations. It removes any recourse the applicant would have should their proprietary information be disclosed by ARIN.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy'. Changing 'reasonable care' to 'minimal care' and eliminating liability contradicts this requirement. The applicant can argue that ARIN failed to take appropriate measures to protect the trade secret, making the agreement unenforceable under Virginia law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 59.1-337. Injunctive relief LIS Session Information Bills & Resolutions State Budget Virginia Law Reports to the General Assembly Virginia Law Code of Virginia All Code of Virginia Administrative Code Constitution Charters Authorities Compacts Uncodified Acts Code of Virginia Table of Contents » Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce » Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act » § 59.1-337. Injunctive relief Creating a Report: Check the sections you'd like to appear in the report, then use the "Create Report" button at the bottom of the page to generate your report. Once the report is generated you'll then have the option to download it as a pdf, print or email the report. Code of Virginia Title 59.1. Trade and Commerce Chapter 26. Uniform Trade Secrets Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-337 . Injunctive relief. A. Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. B. In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. C. In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. 1986, c. 210. The chapters of the acts of assembly referenced in the historical citation at the end of this section may not constitute a comprehensive list of such chapters and may exclude chapters whose provisions have expired. Virginia Law Library The Code of Virginia, Constitution of Virginia, Charters, Authorities, Compacts and Unc
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'reasonable care' to 'minimal care' and removes all exceptions where ARIN is liable for disclosure, weakening the protection of proprietary information. The Law Explanation argues this contradicts the Virginia Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which requires 'reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy'. Snippet 1, from the Virginia Code § 59.1-337, discusses injunctive relief for misappropriation. While it does not explicitly state the 'reasonable efforts' requirement, the Law Explanation provides the basis for the contradiction, indicating the change to 'minimal care' undermines the expected standard of protection under trade secret law, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (1) "Proprietary Information" shall be defined as and limited to, (a) network engineering plans, including subnets, and host counts, and hosts per subnet with projected utilization rates and associated confidence levels of those projections for one and two years in the future; (b) deployment schedules for the network, including major milestones for each subnet; (c) network topology diagrams originated by the Applicant, not previously published or otherwise disclosed to the general public, not previously available without restriction to ARIN or others, and not normally furnished to others without compensation; and (d) such other information which the Applicant desires to protect against unrestricted disclosure or competitive use, and which the parties mutually agree shall be furnished pursuant to this Non-Disclosure Agreement and which is app ropriately identified as being proprietary when initially furnished to ARIN.
perturbation.changed_text: (1) "Proprietary Information" shall be defined as and limited to, (a) any information whatsoever, regardless of its nature or origin; (b) anything the applicant considers confidential; (c) information already in the public domain; and (d) information that ARIN independently develops.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text broadens the definition of 'Proprietary Information' to include any information at all, even information already in the public domain or independently developed by ARIN. This is overly broad and contradicts the legal concept of trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (First) of Torts § 757 comment b specifies that a trade secret must be something that provides a competitive advantage because it is not generally known or readily ascertainable. Including information already in the public domain as 'Proprietary Information' contradicts this fundamental requirement for trade secret protection and creates an unenforceable clause.

perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'Proprietary Information' to include information already in the public domain. The Law Explanation states that Restatement (First) of Torts § 757 comment b specifies that a trade secret must provide a competitive advantage because it is not generally known or readily ascertainable. By including information already in the public domain, the changed text directly contradicts this fundamental requirement for trade secret protection, as it attempts to classify publicly available information as proprietary, which is not legally defensible.

perturbation.original_text: (8) This Non-Disclosure Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Virginia, without regard for Virginia's choice of law rules.
perturbation.changed_text: (8) This Non-Disclosure Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of North Korea, without regard for Virginia's choice of law rules.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to North Korea makes the NDA practically unenforceable in the United States, as North Korean law is inaccessible and incompatible with US legal principles. This undermines the entire agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment
perturbation.law_explanation: Applying North Korean law to a contract between US-based entities may violate the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment, which requires a reasonable connection between the chosen jurisdiction and the parties or subject matter of the contract. Because North Korea has no connection to ARIN or the Applicant, the choice of law is unreasonable, making the agreement suspect.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Fifth Amendment | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Fifth Amendment The Fifth Amendment creates a number of rights relevant to both criminal and civil legal proceedings. In criminal cases , the Fifth Amendment guarantees the right to a grand jury , forbids “ double jeopardy ,” and protects against self-incrimination . It also requires that “ due process of law ” be part of any proceeding that denies a citizen “life, liberty or property” and requires the government to compensate citizens when it takes private property for public use. Learn more... Primary tabs Amendment V No person shall be held to answer for a capital, or otherwise infamous crime, unless on a presentment or indictment of a grand jury, except in cases arising in the land or naval forces, or in the militia, when in actual service in time of war or public danger; nor shall any person be subject for the same offense to be twice put in jeopardy of life or limb; nor shall be compelled in any criminal case to be a witness against himself, nor be deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law; nor shall private property be taken for public use, without just compensation. perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the governing law of Virginia with that of North Korea. The Law Explanation argues this violates the Due Process Clause of the Fifth Amendment because there's no reasonable connection between North Korea and the parties involved. The Perturbation Explanation states that North Korean law is inaccessible and incompatible, making the NDA unenforceable in the US. The provided snippet from the Fifth Amendment confirms that no person shall be deprived of life, liberty, or property, without due process of law. By selecting North Korean law, where due process and fair legal proceedings are not reliably available to US entities, the contract undermines the due process rights of the parties, creating a contradiction.
